Text:
Your first Python program won't teach you anything until you understand this.

Most tutorials skip the actual reason you're learning.

They treat Python like syntax to memorize instead of a thinking tool.

But data science is different. It requires:

→ An isolated environment (so code is reproducible)
→ Computational thinking (break problems, spot patterns, reuse solutions)
→ Your first program touching real data (not Hello World)
→ Error messages treated as feedback (not failure)

Setup matters more than syntax. Thinking matters more than speed.

Tutorial 1 of 10 lays this foundation. Swipe to see the three steps that change everything.

  1. Why Most Tutorials Get It Wrong
  2. Environment Setup: Non-Negotiable
  3. Computational Thinking: The Mental Model
  4. Your First Real Program
  5. Error Messages as Feedback

Text:
Python will give you a wrong answer without telling you.

Not an error. Not a crash. A number that looks right.

It happens when a column of ages loads as strings. Python does not add them — it concatenates. No warning. No flag.

This is why types matter before anything else in data science.

Tutorial 2 covers the four building blocks you need before pandas:

→ int, float, str, bool — and why they behave differently
→ Lists vs dicts — and when to use which
→ List comprehensions — one line instead of five
→ Functions — the thing that makes your code trustworthy

Slide through. Save for when the silent bug finally bites.

Text:
Your Python loop is 200x slower than it needs to be.

NumPy isn't just a library. It's a different way of thinking about data.

Instead of writing loops, you write operations — and NumPy applies them to every element at once.

scores[scores > 90]

That one line is filtering, boolean logic, and vectorization in 19 characters. No loop. No append. Just a description of what you want.

Swipe through to see the mental model shift — from Python loops to array operations. This is Tutorial 3/10 in the Python for Data Science series. Full post linked in bio.
